summary: Find all competing doctrine/style packs, benchmark them head-to-head against no-slop, steal verified strengths

packs.name: adenaufal/anti-slop-writing (english/system-prompt.md)
strengths: Exhaustive, concrete ban list with mechanized replacements: it names specific banned tokens (pivotal, tapestry, delve, 'It's not just X, it's Y', em/en dashes) AND gives the plain-language substitution or restructure for each, so it is directly executable rather than aspirational.

Grounds every rule in a stated mechanism/metric: it ties rules to perplexity (median AI 21.2 vs human 35.9), burstiness (sentence-length variation, cited as reducing detection up to 40%), and stylometry (Turnitin's 31 features), giving a falsifiable 'why' behind each instruction instead of taste assertions.

Ships a closed-loop 24-point post-generation checklist that converts the doctrine into a verification pass (count dashes to zero, find any 3-item list and add/remove one, kill trailing participial clauses), which is a real enforcement mechanism most style guides lack.

Model-specific and detector-specific awareness (EN-11 separates ChatGPT vs Claude vs Gemini tells; the T-1..T-5 section targets Turnitin/GPTZero/Originality.ai behaviors including the Aug-2025 humanizer-detection update), showing it is maintained against a moving adversary rather than written once.

Attacks structure and rhythm, not just vocabulary: burstiness, syntactic-depth variation, paragraph-rhythm irregularity, register code-switching, and function-word diversity are all addressed, so it reaches the deeper stylometric layer where vocabulary-only guides fail.
weaknesses: Optimizes for evading detectors, not for being right or clear: the north star is 'don't get flagged by Turnitin/GPTZero,' so it teaches disguise (add imperfections, fragments, register shifts) rather than judgment about when prose actually serves the reader. A judgment doctrine wins by targeting truth/usefulness; this targets a classifier.

Mechanical rules produce their own detectable tics and can harm quality: mandating fragments, forced register shifts '2-3 per piece,' banned tricolons, and deliberate redundancy 'for rhythm' is cargo-cult humanization. Applied literally it manufactures a new uniform style (anti-slop slop) and degrades genuinely well-structured writing that legitimately needed three examples or an em dash.

Absolute bans ignore context and are sometimes wrong: 'Ban ALL dashes, target ZERO' and 'never default to three' are blanket prohibitions with no room for the case where the dash or the tricolon is the correct choice. Good judgment is conditional; this doctrine hard-codes unconditional rules, which is exactly the brittleness a judgment-based rival should exploit.

Some empirical claims are stated as fact without verifiable citation ('a 2026 study of 1,000+ URLs,' 'reduced detection up to 40% in studies,' specific detector accuracy percentages), which undercuts its own 'name the study / no vague attribution' content rule and makes the metric-grounding partly rhetorical.

It is a banlist plus checklist, not a theory of writing: it enumerates ~200 things to avoid but offers little generative guidance on constructing a good argument, structuring information for a reader, or deciding what to say. A competitor built on positive judgment principles can cover cases this negative-space list never anticipates.

Detector-evasion framing invites an ethics/legitimacy attack: overtly coaching users to beat Turnitin and avoid humanizer-tool detection reads as academic-integrity circumvention, which is a reputational and positioning liability a 'judgment/craft' doctrine does not carry.

packs.name: BioInfo/slopless (rules/writing-voice.md + CLAUDE.md)
strengths: Exhaustive, categorized ban-lists (verbs, intensifiers, poetic abstractions, stock openings, faux-depth closers, mechanical transitions) give a concrete, high-recall surface-slop filter that is easy to enforce and audit line-by-line.

Quantified authenticity/burstiness rules are a genuine mechanism most anti-slop docs lack: explicit thresholds (a sub-8-word and a 20+-word sentence per paragraph, no 3 consecutive 12-18-word sentences, 1-2 fragments per 300 words, vary the first 3-5 words of adjacent openers) make style measurable rather than vibes.

Named structural anti-patterns (forced 'not just X but Y' contrasts, rhetorical Q&A scaffolding, algorithmic rule-of-three, symmetric paragraphs, paragraph-ending profundity, manufactured parallelism) target deep structural tells, not just word choice, which is where most slop lives.
The 5-tier register ladder ties formality to context (Slack -> internal -> exec -> published -> social), so the doctrine adapts output to audience instead of applying one flat voice.

Built-in post-draft self-scan checklist plus anti-fabrication rules ('vague but honest beats specific but invented', never invent names/dates/facts) operationalize enforcement as a verification pass and add a light honesty guardrail.
weaknesses: Scope is style-only: its explicit goal is making text 'indistinguishable from human writing.' It has almost no judgment doctrine, no calibration of confidence, no reasoning about when to push back, disagree, or say 'I don't know' beyond one anti-fabrication line, so it optimizes surface authenticity over substance.

Ban-lists are brittle and gameable: a fixed blocklist of words/phrases invites shallow synonym-swapping (models route around 'delve' to an unlisted equivalent), can produce false positives on legitimate technical uses (e.g. 'robust', 'optimize' in engineering), and needs constant maintenance as tells shift.

Optimizing to be 'undetectable as AI' is an adversarial framing that can conflict with honesty and can degrade clarity; it treats human-mimicry as the terminal goal rather than correctness, usefulness, or truthfulness, and offers no rule for when the mimicry goal should yield.

No mechanism for handling the reader's actual task quality: nothing about evidence standards, sourcing, verifying claims, structuring an argument, or acknowledging uncertainty, so a fluent, human-sounding but wrong answer passes all its checks.

The customizable voice section is thin and user-dependent (mostly TODO scaffolding), and some rules are self-contradictory or hard to enforce jointly (e.g. rigid quantified sentence-length quotas can themselves become a detectable mechanical rhythm, the exact trap the doc warns against).

packs.name: realrossmanngroup/no_ai_slop_writing_rules (CLAUDE.md)
packs.license: No explicit LICENSE file in repo (unspecified / all-rights-reserved by default)
strengths: Concrete self-check enforcement loop: it mandates reading two skill files first and scanning every output against a named references/ai-writing-detection.md list before returning, and applies the rules recursively to its own file and replies. This is an actual mechanism, not an aspiration.

Anti-fabrication rules are unusually sharp and defamation-aware. Rules 17-19 and 23-24 forbid invented case studies, fake dates/milestones, and fabricated attributions (flagging the defamation risk of 'Senator X stated...'), and demand exact quoting with bracketed alterations and set-off block quotes over ~15 words. That is a real epistemic-integrity layer, not just style.

The 'testable detail' principle is a genuine judgment heuristic in disguise: rule 5 (every claim ends in a concrete verifiable detail or gets deleted) and rule 13 ('if a sentence could appear on any generic site unchanged, delete it') give a falsifiable cut-test that generalizes beyond any specific banned word.

No-research-narration rule (24) is a sophisticated, less-common mechanism: it bans 'could not be located', 'as of [date]' self-qualifiers, and enumerations of what couldn't be found, forcing the writer to silently omit unsupported facts rather than perform the search. Most anti-slop packs miss this tell.

Claimed empirical grounding: the voice profile references corpus analysis (sentence-length variance, testable-number density, claim-then-proof structure, contraction and ampersand habits, a statistical fingerprint), so the persona is at least framed as data-derived rather than vibes.
weaknesses: It is fundamentally a blacklist, and blacklists are trivially evadable and self-obsoleting. Rules 20-22 enumerate specific banned words (delve, leverage, furthermore, myriad, plethora); a model can produce fluent slop using none of them, and the ban list ages as model output distributions shift. There is no mechanism that catches novel tells not already on a list.

Weak conflict-resolution and prioritization. The 24 rules are flat and 'non-negotiable' with no tie-break order, no severity tiers, and no guidance when rules collide (e.g. rule 2's no-unsourced-statistics vs a legitimate estimate, or persona demands vs a context that needs neutral prose). A judgment doctrine needs an arbitration layer; this has none.

The load-bearing content is offloaded to files not in this doc. The actual banned-word taxonomy and the statistical voice fingerprint live in .claude/skills/*/SKILL.md and references/ai-writing-detection.md, which are not present here, so as a standalone competitor artifact this CLAUDE.md is a table of contents pointing at the real weapons.

Hard-coded persona limits portability. It bakes in the Louis Rossmann voice (contempt-through-precision, ampersand habit, right-to-repair framing in its own examples). That is a narrow register; for neutral docs, warm marketing, or any non-Rossmann task the persona actively fights the goal, and the doc offers no dial to disengage it.

It legislates prose surface but never defines slop's root cause or a decision procedure. There is no model of *why* text is empty (no reader-value test, no claim-load or information-density metric, no calibration of confidence), so it can pass a defensively-worded, fact-thin paragraph that dodges every banned token yet still says nothing. It polices tells, not judgment.

Self-referential fragility: rule 23 (exact-match quoting) is exactly what caused the WebFetch summarizer to refuse verbatim reproduction, showing the ruleset can deadlock a compliant agent against a legitimate task; a robust doctrine anticipates the verbatim-reproduction vs paraphrase conflict rather than hard-banning one side.

packs.name: jalaalrd/anti-ai-slop-writing (SKILL.md)
strengths: Quantified, measurable thresholds instead of vibes: 'max ONE em dash per 500 words', 'one exclamation per 1000 words', 'never more than 5-7 bullets in a row', 'no three consecutive same-length sentences'. These are checkable, auditable constraints an author (or an evaluator) can literally count, which makes the doctrine enforceable rather than aspirational.

A terminal Self-Check gate: the 11-point numbered checklist run 'before every output' converts the prose rules into an explicit pre-flight pass with a final catch-all ('Sounds like ChatGPT? -> Rewrite until no'). This closes the loop between having rules and actually applying them, which most style docs omit.

Concrete before/after exemplars carry the abstractions: nearly every 'What To Do Instead' rule is anchored by a specific contrast ('you'll run out of USDC in 47 days' beats 'powerful analytics capabilities'; '34 users the first week, 12 came back' beats 'significant growth'). Showing the delta teaches the pattern far better than naming it.

It targets structural tells, not just vocabulary — the genuinely differentiated mechanism. Rule-of-three, parataxis chains, uniform sentence length, identical paragraph architecture (topic->explanation->example->transition), and parallel section structure are the statistical fingerprints most banned-word lists miss. Explicitly naming parataxis and giving a rewrite is unusually sophisticated.

Anti-fabrication rules are woven in as first-class constraints, not an afterthought: 'never invent anecdotes', 'fabricated specificity is worse than honest vagueness', use 'imagine/suppose' for hypotheticals, no fake quotes or statistics. This guards the failure mode where 'sound human' degenerates into 'confidently make things up.'

Context/voice awareness prevents one-size output: the Voice Calibration block ('Does this person swear? What would they NEVER say? Cover letter != tweet != LinkedIn != DM') plus format rules that disable markdown/headers in social and plain-text contexts show it understands register shifts rather than applying a single flattening pass.
weaknesses: It is a surface-pattern eraser, not a judgment engine. Every rule optimizes for 'not detectable as AI' — a defensive, evasion-oriented goal. It has no machinery for deciding whether a claim is true, whether an argument holds, what the strongest counter-case is, or whether the piece should exist at all. A doctrine built on judgment/reasoning out-scopes it entirely; this competes only on the thin styling layer.

Rules are stated as absolute bans with almost no cost/tradeoff reasoning, which makes them brittle and self-contradictory under load. 'No rule of three' and 'no three same-length sentences ever' collide with legitimate content that genuinely has three items or needs rhythmic repetition; 'no passive construction' is wrong for methods sections, legal, and agentless statements. Blanket prohibitions with no override principle produce mangled prose in the hands that follow them literally.

Adversarially fragile / self-defeating at scale. Once 'max one em dash per 500 words' and 'use semicolons because AI underuses them' are published detection heuristics, they become the new tells: text that religiously avoids em dashes and over-deploys semicolons is itself a detectable signature. The doctrine chases a moving target and bakes in 2025-era detector folklore with no mechanism to update as detectors adapt.

No verification or grounding mechanism behind the honesty rules. It commands 'use real verifiable names, companies, dates' and cites 'A Databricks report from March 2026' as a model — but provides no tool, source-check, or process to confirm such a reference is real. It thus encourages confident-sounding specificity while offering nothing to stop the exact fabrication it warns against.

The banned-words core is externalized and unseen: the single most load-bearing rule ('never use any word or phrase on that list') points to references/banned-words.md, which is not in this file. The doctrine's actual teeth are a maintenance-heavy denylist that is unauditable here, likely to date quickly, and prone to false positives (banning ordinary words that happen to be AI-frequent).

It optimizes voice/evasion but has no altitude control or audience-value test. There is no notion of what the reader needs to decide, what to cut for relevance, how to prioritize information, or when brevity beats craft. 'Make it sound human' can be fully satisfied while the piece remains useless — the doctrine cannot tell a well-voiced empty post from a substantive one.

packs.name: hardikpandya/stop-slop (SKILL.md)
strengths: Concrete, matchable pattern blacklists. Rather than abstract advice, it ships literal string patterns ('not X, it's Y', 'the decision emerges', 'Here's the thing:') paired with a specific fix for each. This makes it directly actionable and near-mechanical to apply or even lint for, which is exactly why it spreads as a copy-paste system-prompt block.

Named taxonomy of AI tells with causal reasoning. It doesn't just ban patterns, it explains WHY (e.g. 'False Agency' — inanimate nouns get human verbs 'because it avoids naming the actor'; negative listing is a 'rhetorical striptease'). Giving each failure mode a name and a mechanism makes the rules teachable and generalizable beyond the exact listed strings.

Self-scoring rubric with a decision threshold. The 5-dimension table (Directness, Rhythm, Trust, Authenticity, Density) rated 1-10 with an explicit 'below 35/50: revise' gate turns subjective 'good writing' into a repeatable pass/fail check the model can run on its own output before delivering.

Pre-delivery checklist as an enforcement step. The 12-item 'Quick Checks' list is framed as a gate to run 'before delivering prose', converting the doctrine from passive advice into an active self-review pass — a lightweight verification loop rather than hope-it-sticks guidance.

Tiered file structure (SKILL.md kernel + references/ detail). The short SKILL.md holds the 8 rules and links out to phrases.md/structures.md/examples.md for the exhaustive lists. This keeps the always-loaded context small while making the full blacklist available on demand — sensible token economy and a clean kernel/expansion split.
weaknesses: Purely stylistic — no judgment, calibration, or epistemics. It governs how sentences look, never whether the underlying claim is true, hedged correctly, or worth making. It cannot catch confident-but-wrong content, sycophancy, false certainty, or a failure to push back. A judgment/anti-slop doctrine needs a reasoning layer this document entirely lacks.

Rigid absolute bans invite overcorrection and new tells. 'Kill ALL adverbs', 'No em dashes at all', 'Two items beat three', 'restructure ANY Wh- opener' are context-blind. Blanket-applied, they degrade legitimate prose (technical writing, quotes, lists that genuinely have three items) and produce a recognizable stripped, choppy over-edited voice — a new slop signature, ironically.

Blacklist is brittle and gameable. It matches specific surface strings, so models trivially route around it with unlisted synonyms ('the system gives rise to' instead of 'the decision emerges'). Maintaining a finite phrase list is a losing arms race; it treats symptoms (tells) rather than the generative cause (the model reaching for filler when it has nothing specific to say).

Self-scoring is unanchored and unauditable. The 1-10 rubric has no anchors, no examples per score, and no calibration, so 'below 35/50' is whatever the model feels — it can rate its own slop a comfortable 40 and ship. There's no independent verification, no adversarial check, no ground truth.

Narrow scope: prose-only, no task/format/honesty coverage. It says nothing about structure of an answer, when to ask vs. assume, admitting uncertainty, refusing a bad framing, or matching effort to stakes. It's a copy-editor, not a doctrine of how an agent should think and respond — so it complements a judgment doctrine at best and cannot substitute for one.

packs.name: harshaneel/humanize
strengths: Mechanistic signal->lever->grader traceability: every one of the nine rewrite levers is explicitly mapped to a graded signal (A-I) in a companion ai-check, so each edit has a named target and a way to be scored. This closes the loop between 'what to change' and 'how you know it worked' far more tightly than a prose style guide.

Unusually deep, concrete anti-pattern catalog for rhetorical scaffolding (Signal I): it names and gives fix-recipes for patterns most style guides never articulate: chiasmus, anaphora, asyndeton tricolon, parallel-subject mirrors, mini-aphorism paragraph closers, copula-avoidance ('serves as'/'stands as'), significance inflation, strawman 'not X, it's Y' pivots. This is the exact 'sounds like insight but is empty' slop a judgment doctrine cares about, catalogued at high resolution.

Staged enforcement protocol with an anti-rationalization guard: it separates a cheap pre-output gate (em dashes, semicolons, banned words) from a deeper audit pass that loops, and it explicitly names the excuses used to keep slop ('removing it would collapse the paragraph', 'the register needs it') and rules them out-of-order. Encoding 'flagged residuals must be removed, not justified' is a genuine mechanism against self-serving retention.

Correct primary insight that current detectors fire on RLHF/instruction-tuning voice, not raw model output (Lever 9, cited to base-model-vs-detector work). Targeting helpful-assistant register, symmetric tradeoffs, unrequested enumeration, appended caveats, and sycophantic prefixes overlaps almost exactly with the highest-value targets of an anti-slop doctrine, and it's argued from evidence rather than taste.

Refuses to fabricate specificity and hard-stops on empty input: the protocol counts factual anchors (numbers, names, dates) and, if zero, STOPS and emits a meta-prompt instead of producing clean-but-empty prose. It also adds a length-honesty rule ('don't pad back up to input length') with a visible meta-note. This is a real integrity mechanism, not just a style pass, and it aligns with a judgment doctrine's stance that vague claims should not be dressed up.
weaknesses: Optimizes the wrong objective for a judgment doctrine: the stated goal is to 'mirror the statistical fingerprint of human writing' and 'avoid AI detection', not to make claims that are true, calibrated, or worth asserting. It edits surface form and can make a false or shallow claim read more confidently human ('direct assertion otherwise', 'pick a side, state your position'). A judgment/anti-slop doctrine owns the truth/worth axis; this owns the texture axis, and it can actively work against the former.

Adversarial / detector-evasion framing is a liability, not an asset, for a credibility-oriented product: it cites TPR-reduction numbers, a 'laundering region', best-of-N against GPTZero/Pangram, and frames homoglyph injection only as a 'dead end' because it's defeated, not because it's dishonest. The whole posture is 'defeat the classifier', which is reputationally and ethically distinct from 'write with judgment' and invites the exact 'this is a cheating tool' critique.

Rigid, count-based rules will misfire and flatten legitimate voice: hard caps like 'max one em dash per 300 words', 'treat every semicolon as a bug', 'std dev of sentence length > 8', 'one sentence <=6 words per 150 words' are stylometric targets, not judgment. Enforced literally they produce their own uniform tell (contrived burstiness, banned-word avoidance artifacts) and punish correct formal/technical prose where semicolons and long sentences are right.

The banned-word list is brittle and gameable, and treats vocabulary as the problem rather than emptiness: proscribing 'delve, leverage, robust, crucial, comprehensive, fundamentally, the real question is' as 'compile-time errors' bans words that are often the correct word. A judgment doctrine would attack the empty claim behind the word; this attacks the token, which both over-triggers and lets genuinely slop content pass as long as it dodges the wordlist.

Heavy dependence on model-in-the-loop scoring and a companion ai-check that isn't in this file: the strongest claims (detector-scored best-of-N, self-rewrite distance, embedding-guided swaps, the A-I grader the levers map to) are optional or live in tooling not shipped here, so the standalone doc is a large rule/checklist bundle whose verification loop is aspirational. Several citations (e.g. arXiv 2605.19516, 2511.00416, TMLR 2026) are future/implausible-dated, so the empirical grounding is partly decorative rather than checkable.

packs.name: blader/humanizer
strengths: Concrete pattern catalog with worked rewrites: 33 named tells, each paired with a 'words to watch' trigger list and a real Before/After example. This makes the rules operational and testable rather than vague ('avoid AI-isms'), and it doubles as few-shot demonstrations a model can pattern-match against.

Explicit false-positive defense: a whole 'What NOT to flag' section lists ~12 things (perfect grammar, formal vocabulary, curly quotes alone, a single em dash, unsourced claims) that are NOT AI tells, plus a 'signs of human writing to preserve' list. This directly targets over-correction, the classic failure mode of anti-slop rules, and insists on clusters-of-tells over isolated hits.

A hard, checkable constraint with a verification step: the em/en-dash rule is framed as non-negotiable and pairs with an explicit final scan ('scan it for the character; any hit means the draft isn't done'). This is a self-audit mechanism, not just an aspiration.

Anti-sterility 'Personality and Soul' section: it recognizes that voiceless clean prose is its own tell, and gates that advice on register (blog/essay vs. encyclopedic/legal), so it doesn't inject fake opinions into reference text. This is more sophisticated than one-directional 'remove patterns' tools.

Voice-matching mechanism: an optional calibration step that reads a user's writing sample first and replaces AI patterns with the sample's own habits (sentence length, diction level, punctuation tics) rather than a generic 'natural' default, plus a draft-audit-final loop that forces a self-critique pass ('what makes this obviously AI generated?').
weaknesses: Purely surface/lexical, not a judgment doctrine: it operates entirely on style tells (word choice, punctuation, rhythm). It has no mechanism for whether a claim is true, whether the reasoning holds, whether the answer is relevant, or whether the content is actually useful. Slop with substance failures passes clean; a well-reasoned draft with an em dash fails. It optimizes for undetectability, not correctness.

Keyword blocklists invite mechanical over-correction and an arms race: banning specific words (delve, tapestry, vibrant, crucial) and constructions trains toward evasion of a fixed list rather than good judgment. The list is static and will date quickly, and a writer/model can satisfy it while still producing hollow prose (Goodhart's law on the tells themselves).

The hard em-dash ban is a blunt instrument stated as doctrine: em dashes are legitimate punctuation many strong human writers use heavily, and the file even concedes this in its own false-positives list, then overrides it with a zero-tolerance rule. This is an internal contradiction and will degrade otherwise correct prose to satisfy a detector-evasion heuristic.

No severity ranking, prioritization, or cost model: all 33 patterns are presented flat, with no guidance on which matter most, when a fix trades away clarity, or when to stop editing. A judgment doctrine needs to weigh tradeoffs; this reads as a lint ruleset where every rule fires equally.

Provenance ceiling and adversarial framing: it is explicitly a distillation of one Wikipedia essay ('Signs of AI writing') aimed at encyclopedic cleanup, so its examples skew toward Wikipedia-style topics and its goal is evading AI detection rather than reasoning well. It offers no defense against a reader/grader who probes logic, evidence, or usefulness rather than style.

packs.name: conorbronsdon/avoid-ai-writing
strengths: Empirically grounded and self-aware: it cites real research (Stanford/Patterns 2023 false-positive rates, BFI 2025, arXiv paraphrase-evasion) and explicitly frames its own flags as 'signals, not proof,' warning against consequential decisions and second-language false positives - intellectual honesty most slop-detectors lack.

Tiered vocabulary system with false-positive control: three tiers (always-flag / flag-in-clusters / flag-by-density) plus inflected-form matching and explicit carve-outs (named-contrast exceptions, self-reference escape hatch) reduce blunt keyword-banning into something context-sensitive.

Two-axis profile architecture: independent context profiles (linkedin/blog/technical-blog/investor-email/docs/casual) and voice profiles (casual/professional/technical/warm/blunt) with a concrete tolerance matrix and stricter-of-the-two composition rule - a genuinely engineered strictness model, not a vibe.

Deep catalogue of structural and fingerprint rules that go beyond word-swapping: paragraph-reshuffle immunity, treadmill/low-information-density tests, sentence-length burstiness, type-token-ratio stylometry, plus mechanical fingerprint catches (citeturn tokens, utm_source=chatgpt.com, unfilled [Your Name] placeholders) that are near-proof of paste-without-editing.

Operationally complete: severity tiers (P0/P1/P2) for triage, an explicit iterate-to-convergence cap, prescribed four-section output with a mandatory self-critique second pass, and edit-in-place discipline (minimal targeted edits, don't touch quotes/code/attributed text) - it is production-ready, not a sketch.
weaknesses: It optimizes for surface statistics, not judgment: the whole system detects and removes the *shape* of AI text (word frequencies, rhythm, TTR) but never teaches the model to reason about whether a claim is true, load-bearing, or worth making - a slop sentence with none of the banned words sails through, and honest prose that happens to use 'robust' or an em-dash gets penalized.

Adversarially self-defeating by its own admission: it cites that paraphrase evades detectors by ~88%, which means a doctrine built on evading those same statistical tells is teaching writing to beat a classifier, not to be good - it can launder confident nonsense into human-sounding confident nonsense.

Maintenance-fragile blacklist: ~100+ hardcoded words/phrases and heavily crypto/web3/DePIN-specific boilerplate rules will date quickly as models shift vocabulary; it is a treadmill of pattern-chasing rather than a durable principle, and the model can't generalize to a new tell it hasn't been enumerated.

No epistemic or decision layer: nothing about calibration of actual confidence, sourcing standards, admitting uncertainty honestly, or refusing to answer - it cuts 'Experts believe' as a *style* tell but offers no mechanism to make the writer actually find and cite the expert, so the cure is often 'delete the hedge' rather than 'do the work.'

Risk of homogenizing toward a single 'blunt/concrete' house style: despite the over-polishing warning, applying ~50 rules plus voice enforcement pushes all output toward the same terse, number-dropping register - it can strip legitimate register (formal, ornate, or genre prose) as aggressively as it strips slop, mistaking voice for a defect.

proposals.mechanism: A find-replace pass on verbose multi-word constructions that inflate simple ideas: 'in order to'→'to', 'due to the fact that'→'because', 'has the ability to'→'can', 'at this point in time'→'now', 'despite the fact that'→'although'. This is wordiness-inflation, a distinct AI tell from the stock-lexicon vocabulary the doctrine already bans.
proposals.anchor: the stock lexicon (delve, showcase, foster, vibrant, crucial, tapestry, landscape)
proposals.insertion: ; and phrase-level padding that inflates a plain word into a clause ("in order to" for "to," "due to the fact that" for "because," "has the ability to" for "can," "at this point in time" for "now") — the fix is the short form, not a synonym for the long one
proposals.why: no-slop bans slop vocabulary (C11 lexicon) and slop performance (C5), and C2 argues brevity comes from selection — but nothing in the file names verbose-construction inflation, where the words are individually plain yet the phrase is three times longer than the idea. harshaneel and bioinfo both ship this as a concrete table; it is a real, checkable class of tell the doctrine currently walks past. Plausibly part of harshaneel's P7-copy edge (10 vs 9.25), since copy is where padding most visibly bloats.

verdict.reasoning:
ANCHOR verified: "the stock lexicon (delve, showcase, foster, vibrant, crucial, tapestry, landscape)" appears verbatim and uniquely in C11 (line 119).

NOVELTY: The insertion names a class the doctrine currently walks past. C11 catalogues lexical/structural tells (word choice, "serves as"/"boasts", not-just-X, triads, bold, em-dashes, rules, closers, tails) — none targets multi-word circumlocutions where every individual word is plain ("in order to," "due to the fact that," "has the ability to," "at this point in time"), so these slip past a lexicon scan. C2 ("Readable beats short — selection gets both") governs a different axis: what whole clauses/ideas to include vs. drop, and it explicitly warns against over-compression — it does not name the case where a retained idea costs three times the words it needs. C5 bans performed stances, not padding phrases. So verbose-construction inflation is genuinely unnamed and is a real, checkable tell.

NO CONTRADICTION: It reinforces C2/C11. Its "the fix is the short form, not a synonym for the long one" clause directly echoes C11's existing "Synonym-swapping fixes none" — consistent.

NOT COPIED: The four example pairs are the field-standard circumlocution list (generic, not proprietary competitor text); the framing is original wording, not lifted from harshaneel/bioinfo tables.

WORDING: Real but wordy/misplaced. Grafting it as a semicolon-appendage onto the single-word stock-lexicon item muddles two distinct tell-types. It reads cleaner as its own tell, and "phrase-level padding that inflates a plain word into a clause" compresses to the single word "circumlocutions." See revised_insertion.
verdict.revised_insertion: circumlocutions that inflate a plain word into a clause ("in order to" for "to," "due to the fact that" for "because," "has the ability to" for "can," "at this point in time" for "now") — the fix is the short form, not a synonym for the long one

proposals.mechanism: Naming one referent through a rotating cast of synonyms to avoid repetition ('the protagonist / the main character / the central figure / the hero' for the same person; 'the company / the firm / the organization / the enterprise'). The fix: pick the canonical noun, keep it, and vary with a pronoun instead. This is a repetition-penalty artifact, a machine tell distinct from every item already in C11.
proposals.insertion: ; one referent renamed through a rotation of synonyms to dodge repeating it ("the protagonist… the central figure… the hero" for the same person) when the canonical noun plus a pronoun is what a writer would use
proposals.why: C11 already catches copula-dodging ('serves as'/'boasts') and importance inflation, but synonym-cycling — the same subject relabeled every sentence — is a separate, high-recall AI fingerprint absent from the list. It belongs exactly where copula-dodging sits because both are 'reaching for a fancier word than the plain one.' Adding it closes a named gap without changing the rule underneath (claims/words sized to what they are).

verdict.reasoning:
The anchor ("serves as"/"boasts" dodging plain "is"/"has") exists verbatim and uniquely in COMPACT.md C11 (line 119), so the anchor test passes. But the insertion fails the "adds behavior no-slop lacks" bar, and the WHY's central claim is false.

The WHY asserts synonym-cycling is "absent from the list." It is not. The companion TELLS.md — which C11 explicitly delegates its full tell-list to ("if the companion TELLS.md sits beside this doctrine, run its sweep instead") and which sits right beside COMPACT.md — already catalogs this exact behavior by its standard name in section 2 (Sentence-shape tells), lines 52-54: "Elegant variation. Refusing to repeat a word — the report becomes 'the document,' then 'the analysis,' then 'the publication,' all one thing. Repeating the right word is clarity; the thesaurus walk is the tell."

That is precisely the proposed insertion ("one referent renamed through a rotation of synonyms to dodge repeating it") and precisely the competitor's Lever 1, which even shares the name "elegant variation." The doctrine's TELLS.md example ("the report... the document... the analysis... the publication") is structurally identical to the proposed example ("the protagonist... the central figure... the hero"). So no-slop already possesses this behavior with equal or better specificity — it also states the fix (repeat the right word), which the insertion does not.

The insertion is therefore redundant with existing coverage, not a new capability. Adding it to C11's tell-list would also duplicate what TELLS.md carries, cutting against the doctrine's own D2 (additions must earn permanence) — the tell-list in COMPACT.md is deliberately a compressed pointer to TELLS.md, not a mirror of it. Not real.

That leaves a real gap: the doctrine can produce one flat 'senior-collaborator' voice across a board memo and a Slack update. So the doctrine can currently produce one flat register (formal, crisp) across a board memo and a Slack update, stripping furniture from both while leaving both equally stiff. The insertion closes that: match the formality/contraction-density/fragment-tolerance the destination actually uses.
